nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and still waters of river margins, oxbow lakes, and floodplain pools within the Amazon and Orinoco basins; typically among submerged roots, woody debris, and dense marginal vegetation where it holds station waiting for prey

decorPreferences: large pieces of driftwood and submerged branches for cover and ambush sites
dim or broken lighting with floating plants (Amazon frogbit, water lettuce) to diffuse surface light
robust marginal plants (Echinodorus, Vallisneria) or artificial equivalents
smooth rock or flat slate to serve as a potential spawning substrate
open midwater swimming corridors between cover zones
dark substrate (fine sand or fine gravel) to complement natural colouration

diet.notes: Obligate sit-and-wait piscivore in the wild; oversized eyes are an adaptation for low-light ambush hunting of fish prey. In captivity, weaning onto non-live foods can be challenging: begin with live feeder fish or live earthworms, then transition to large frozen foods (whole silversides, lance fish, river shrimp, large bloodworm clusters). High-quality large cichlid pellets or carnivore sticks may eventually be accepted once the fish is settled. Avoid a diet of solely goldfish feeder fish due to thiaminase concerns; varied frozen foods are preferable once the fish is feeding reliably.

goodWith: similarly-sized robust South American cichlids kept in spacious tanks with visual barriers (e.g., large Cichla in public-aquarium setups)
large, armoured catfish (Pseudoplatystoma, large Pimelodus) too big to be eaten
large Loricariidae (Panaque, large Hypostomus) as bottom-level tankmates
a single conspecific bonded pair in a dedicated species tank of 400 L+
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed whole (typically under 12–15 cm)
slow or docile fish that cannot escape ambush attempts
small tetras, rasboras, barbs, and similar community fish
dwarf cichlids and other small cichlids
conspecifics except a proven bonded pair — severe aggression between rival adults

Wikimedia Commons candidate; verify the file exists at this path — the species is infrequently photographed and Commons holdings may differ.

summary: A large, rarely traded Amazonian ambush predator with dramatically oversized eyes adapted for low-light hunting. Reaches ~25 cm and demands a 300 L+ species or near-species tank. Intermediate-to-advanced keepers only; weaning off live prey can test patience. Best kept singly or as a bonded pair.
careNotes:
Acaronia nassa is an uncommonly encountered cichlid in the hobby, occasionally imported from South American wild-caught collections. Its most distinctive feature — the greatly enlarged eyes — is a functional adaptation for detecting prey movement in murky, dimly lit Amazonian floodplain habitats rather than a purely ornamental trait. The species employs a sit-and-wait hunting strategy, holding station in cover and striking at passing fish with a rapid lunge; its mouth is large and protrusible in proportion to body size.

A tank of at least 300 L with a minimum 150 cm footprint is necessary for a single adult. The décor should replicate the dark, heavily shaded margins of Amazonian lakes and flooded forest: large driftwood tangles, submerged branches, floating plants to reduce surface light, and a dark fine-sand substrate. Bright, open aquascapes cause chronic stress and suppress normal behaviour. Water should be soft and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 5.5–7.5, gH 1–12) and warm (24–29 °C), with excellent filtration maintained since large carnivores produce substantial waste; weekly water changes of 25–30% are important.

Feeding is the principal challenge with new specimens. Wild-caught individuals almost invariably arrive expecting live prey, and patience is required to wean them onto frozen alternatives such as whole silversides, lance fish, and large river shrimp, followed eventually by large carnivore pellets. Reliance on goldfish feeder fish is discouraged due to thiaminase content and disease-transmission risk.

Distinguish from the closely related A. vultuosa (Black-tongued Acaronia, sometimes called the Vulture Cichlid), which is more boldly marked — displaying stronger lateral banding and a dark-pigmented oral mucosa — and originates from the upper Rio Negro and Orinoco drainages. The two species should not be confused in trade; A. nassa is the more commonly imported of the two but both remain rare.

Conspecific aggression between adults is severe outside a bonded pair. A paired male and female can be kept together in 400 L+ with substantial structure to break sightlines; unpaired individuals should be housed singly. Tankmates, if any, must be too large to be prey items and robust enough to avoid harassment.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is documented but infrequent, reflecting the species' rarity in the hobby. Acaronia nassa is a substrate-spawner: a bonded pair will clean a flat, smooth surface (slate, broad smooth stone, or a firm leaf) and deposit adhesive eggs that both parents guard aggressively. Clutch sizes of several hundred eggs have been reported. Both parents participate in fry guarding and may move wriggling larvae to pre-dug pits in the substrate. Water temperature at the warmer end of the range (27–29 °C), soft acidic water, and complete absence of tankmate disturbance are prerequisites for successful spawning. Fry are initially fed on live baby brine shrimp and micro-worms; transition to finely chopped frozen foods as they grow. Parents can become extremely aggressive post-spawn and may kill any other fish in the tank.

additional.name: Seriously Fish — Acaronia vultuosa (comparison species)
additional.notes: Consulted to accurately distinguish A. nassa from the related A. vultuosa (Black-tongued Acaronia): bolder lateral markings, dark oral mucosa, upper Rio Negro / Orinoco origin.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Cichlid Care Overview
additional.notes: General cichlid husbandry principles consulted for compatibility framing and diet weaning guidance; no dedicated Acaronia nassa page exists on Aquarium Co-Op.

fish.breedingNotes: Substrate-spawner; bonded pair cleans a flat surface and deposits adhesive eggs guarded by both parents. Requires soft acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5), 27–29 °C, and a species-only tank. Fry: live baby brine shrimp initially, then chopped frozen foods. Rarely achieved in captivity; documented but infrequent.
